1: I made up my mind not to make another painful visit to you — the painful visit (en lypē) refers to Paul's second visit to Corinth (the first was the founding visit of Acts 18:1-18). This second visit is mentioned in 2 Corinthians 13:2 and was apparently marked by conflict with a particular person (2 Corinthians 2:5-11).
2: For if I cause you pain, who is there to make me glad but the one whom I have pained? — the pastoral logic: the apostle's joy is bound up with the congregation's joy. This reflects the symbiotic relationship between teacher and community in ancient Mediterranean culture.
3: And I wrote as I did, so that when I come I might not suffer pain from those who should have made me rejoice — the tearful letter (2 Corinthians 2:4) is not 1 Corinthians but a now-lost intermediate letter, probably written after the painful visit and before 2 Corinthians. Its severe tone produced a period of estrangement.
4: For I wrote to you out of much affliction and anguish of heart and with many tears, not to cause you pain but to let you know the abundant love that I have for you — the emotional register of this description (thlipsis, synocha kardias, dakryon pollon — affliction, anguish of heart, many tears) is unparalleled in Paul's letters.
5: Now if anyone has caused pain, he has caused it not to me, but in some measure — not to put it too severely — to all of you — the offender (ho adikēsas in 2 Corinthians 2:10) is usually identified with the person who led opposition to Paul during the painful visit. The indefinite reference (tis — someone) may be diplomatic or may reflect that the identity was well known to the Corinthians.
6: For such a one, this punishment by the majority is enough — the community discipline (epitimia, censure, punishment) exercised by to pleion (the majority) suggests a formal congregational action, possibly expulsion or shaming, that Paul now says is sufficient.
7: So you should rather turn to forgive and comfort him, or he may be overwhelmed by excessive sorrow — the pastoral concern for the over-punished offender mirrors the later rabbinic principle that punishment must not reduce a person to shame beyond recovery.
8: So I beg you to reaffirm your love for him — the verb kyroō (ratify, confirm) is a legal term used for validating a decision or contract. Paul calls for an official communal act of love-reaffirmation to counterbalance the official censure.
9: For this is why I wrote, that I might test (eis to gnōnai, to know; dokimēn, proof through testing) you and know whether you are obedient in everything — the tearful letter was partly a test of whether the congregation would submit to apostolic authority. The test has been passed (2 Corinthians 7:14-16).
10: Anyone whom you forgive, I also forgive — the apostolic forgiveness pronounced by Paul mirrors the authority Jesus granted to his disciples (John 20:23: if you forgive the sins of anyone, they are forgiven). What I have forgiven, if I have forgiven anything, has been for your sake in the presence of Christ.
11: So that we would not be outwitted by Satan — the strategic awareness of satanic schemes (noēmata, designs, intentions) reflects the early Christian understanding that community divisions served demonic purposes. Paul's concern for reconciliation is therefore spiritual warfare.
12: When I came to Troas to preach the gospel of Christ, even though a door was opened for me in the Lord — the open-door metaphor for missionary opportunity (1 Corinthians 16:9; Colossians 4:3). Troas (Alexandria Troas) was an important port city on the northwest coast of Asia Minor, a major transit point between Macedonia and Asia.
13: My spirit was not at rest because I did not find my brother Titus there — Titus is Paul's trusted emissary to Corinth, mentioned only in 2 Corinthians and the Pastoral Epistles in the Pauline corpus. Paul's anxiety about Titus reflects the intense pastoral investment in the Corinthian situation.
14: But thanks be to God, who in Christ always leads us in triumphal procession (thriambeuonti) — the Roman triumph (triumphus) was the ceremonial procession granted to a general who had won a major victory. The triumphator rode in a chariot through Rome with his captives, his troops, spoils, and incense-burning priests. Paul uses this imperial spectacle to describe apostolic mission.
15: For we are the aroma of Christ to God among those who are being saved and among those who are perishing — in the Roman triumph, incense was burned throughout the procession. To the victorious Romans, the fragrance signified celebration and life; to the captives being led to execution, the same fragrance signaled death. Paul applies the dual symbolism to the gospel.
16: To one a fragrance from death to death, to the other a fragrance from life to life — the same message produces diametrically opposite effects depending on the hearer's orientation. Who is sufficient (hikanos) for these things? — the rhetorical question sets up chapter 3's answer: our sufficiency is from God (2 Corinthians 3:5).
17: For we are not, like so many, peddlers (kapeleuontes, hucksters, adulterators) of God's word — the kapēlos was a small trader often associated with adulteration of goods for profit. The contrast is with the sincere (eilikrineia) and God-commissioned messenger. The accusation of kapēleia (peddling) was a standard charge against Sophists who charged fees for their teaching.
